1: Welcome. This is a short and calm walkthrough about breathing and relaxation, what is sometimes called prophylaxis. These are tools you practise in advance and carry with you into the birth.
2: Prophylaxis is nothing complicated. It is breathing, relaxation, and focus that you practise at home, so your body knows them when it counts. The tools don't take away all the pain, but they make it less frightening and easier to meet.
3: The breathing is the heart of prophylaxis. Breathe in through the nose, and blow out slowly through the mouth, a little longer out than in. The calm out-breath tells your body it is safe, so it relaxes. Take one contraction at a time, a few breaths at a time, and rest between the waves.
4: A tense body hurts more and fights against it. When you consciously let go of the tension you let the contractions do their work, and you save your strength. Drop your shoulders down from your ears, loosen your jaw, and open your hands. Go through the body from the top down between contractions.
5: When it gets intense it helps to have somewhere to direct your attention. Rest your gaze on a fixed point, let the partner count and breathe with you, and think only of the contraction you are in, not the next one. As soon as the contraction releases, let go of all the focus too and rest.
6: This pain is not a sign that something is wrong. Each contraction opens the womb a little more and brings you closer to your baby. It has a direction and an end. You are doing nothing wrong, you are doing a job.
7: Partner, you cannot take the contraction, but you can hold the calm. Breathe visibly and audibly so that the mother has a rhythm to follow, count the pace through every contraction, and remind her gently to let go of her shoulders and jaw. Between contractions you hand her water, wipe her forehead, and keep the room calm.
8: This works best if the body already knows it. Lie down comfortably, put on the audio-guided exercise, and breathe through it together a few times before the birth. Short sessions a few times a week are enough. Then the partner can lead the breathing the same way on the postnatal ward.
9: How safe you feel affects how much it hurts. Dimmed light, calm sound, familiar things, and few people in the room help your body's own calming hormones. Do change position, rock, or take a warm shower. It is completely fine to ask the midwife for help to dim the room.
10: You breathe your way through this, breath by breath. A long calm out-breath, a soft body, one contraction at a time, and the partner by your side. Do ask your questions.
